response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), in force since May 2018, applies to any organization processing EU residents’ data and guarantees rights such as access, erasure, portability and explicit consent, while mandating Data Protection Officers and 72-hour breach notifications. Non-compliance can draw penalties of up to 4 % of global annual revenue or €20 million, illustrated by record fines of €1.2 billion for Meta, €746 million for Amazon and more than €200 million for Google. GDPR has become the model for privacy laws worldwide, inspiring California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act. Although it elevates data privacy to a fundamental right, critics warn that complex compliance rules, uneven enforcement and ubiquitous cookie-consent banners burden small businesses and frustrate users.
